title: Kedgeree
subtitle: Smoked haddock, basmati rice, boiled egg, curry spice.
excerpt: Smoked haddock poached gently in milk, flaked into curry-spiced rice, finished with quartered boiled eggs and chopped coriander. The Anglo-Indian breakfast brought back from the Raj in the late nineteenth century. Forty minutes from start to plate.
sourceNotes: Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845, Project Gutenberg), records the dish under its Anglo-Indian form. The original khichuri of Bengal is a rice-and-lentil dish; the British version added smoked fish and dropped the lentil.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The eggs can be boiled and the fish poached up to a few hours ahead, kept refrigerated. The rice cooks fresh.

content.text: Three components, three small pans. The smoked haddock poaches in milk and bay for ten minutes. The eggs go in cold water and come to a soft-boiled six minutes. The rice cooks in onion that has been softened with cumin, turmeric, and a teaspoon of mild curry powder. The three components meet at the end: rice tipped into a wide pan, fish flaked over, eggs quartered and laid on top, parsley and lemon to finish.
Undyed smoked haddock has a paler, softer flavour and looks closer to the dish's origins. The bright yellow-dyed kind works too, with a stronger smoke and a more dramatic colour.

Three pans on the hob: one wide saucepan or sauté pan for the rice, one small saucepan for the fish, one small saucepan for the eggs. A slotted fish slice or wide spoon for lifting the fish out of the milk.

Lay {{smoked-haddock}} of haddock in a saucepan large enough to take the fish flat. Pour over {{whole-milk}} of milk and 200 ml of water. Add {{bay-leaves}} of bay and {{black-pepper}} of peppercorns. Bring slowly to a bare simmer over low heat — bubbles should just break the surface. Cook 8 minutes. Lift the fish out with a slotted spoon onto a plate. Strain the poaching liquid through a fine sieve and reserve 500 ml; you'll use it for the rice.
Lower {{eggs}} of eggs gently into a small pan of simmering water. Cook for 6 minutes for jammy yolks, 8 for firm. Tip into cold water at once. Peel when cool enough to handle. Cut in quarters.

Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in the wide saucepan over medium heat. Add {{cumin-seeds}} of cumin seeds and {{cardamom-green}} of bruised cardamom. Let them sizzle 10 seconds. Tip in {{onion}} of chopped onion. Cook 8 minutes until soft. Stir in {{curry-powder}} of curry powder and {{turmeric}} of turmeric for 30 seconds.

Stir in {{basmati-rice}} of rinsed rice to coat every grain in butter and spice. Pour in 500 ml of the strained poaching milk. Bring to a simmer, cover, drop the heat to its lowest, and cook 15 minutes. Take off the heat and rest, covered, 5 minutes.

Skin the haddock and flake into generous chunks, checking for any pin bones. Stir gently through the rice. If using cream, fold it in now. Tip into a warm serving dish. Lay the quartered eggs on top. Scatter with {{parsley-flat}} of chopped parsley. Serve with wedges of {{lemon}} of lemon and the salt-and-pepper mill at the table.

items.fix: Keep the milk at a bare simmer — bubbles just breaking the surface. The fish poaches gently in 8 minutes, no more.

items.cause: Lid lifted during cooking, or too much liquid.
items.fix: 500 ml of liquid to 300 g of rice. Don't lift the lid until the 15-minute timer goes, plus the 5-minute rest.

items.cause: Curry powder not bloomed, or fish under-poached.
items.fix: Cook the spices for the full 30 seconds in butter before liquid goes in. Use undyed haddock with proper smoke; supermarket dyed kind tastes flatter.
Six minutes from simmering water gives soft-jammy yolks. Plunge into cold water at once to stop the cooking.

content.text: Kedgeree was the British breakfast brought back from colonial India — a misreading of the Bengali khichuri, a humble rice-and-lentil porridge eaten across the subcontinent. The Anglo-Indian re-invention added smoked fish, eggs, and the British breakfast clock. It belongs to the country-house Saturday morning of Edwardian England and to weekend brunch in any British household with smoked haddock in the fridge.
